This Section deals with a few more complex structures of this kind.

Cooked in a slow oven, it will taste delicious, (participle clause) She ran out laughing like a hyena, (participle clause) I'd like to look at those papers again, (infinitive clause) Infinitives, -ing forms and participles can all begin clauses.

and Past Participles: Section 10  Infinitives, -ing forms grammar •  111    infinitives after question words: who to ..., etc To switch on, press red button.

He went abroad to forget. I'm going to Austria to learn German.

I sat down to rest. (     I sat down for resting / for to rest.) something.

We often use an infinitive to talk about a person's purpose - why  he or she does 1 I sat    down       to   rest.

112  infinitive       of  purpose:     I sat    down      to   rest.    _ For questions beginning Why (not) + infinitive, ►    630.

WHAT    TO  DO  IF FIRE  BREAKS   OUT HOW   TO  IMPROVE    YOUR    PRONUNCIATION leaflets, books, etc. (Note: these are not questions.) How to ..., What to ..., etc are often found as titles for instructions, information 3  titles:   What      to   do   if  fire    breaks        out.

Who should I pay? (     Wh o to pay?) not What shall we do? (     What to do?) not How shall I tell her? (     Hew to tell her?) After question words, we  often use shall and should.

We   do not usually begin a direct question with How to...?, What to ...?, etc.

2 direct     questions:      What      shall      we    do?

(BUT NOT    I can't understand why to do it.) I can't decide whether to answer her email.

Tell me when to pay.

I don't know where to put the car.

Can you show me how to get to the station? (= ... how  ɪ  can get to Tell us what to do.

I wonder who to invite. (= ... who  I should invite.) as obligation and possibility.

who, what, where, etc (but not usually why). This structure expresses ideas such In indirect speech (►  262.2), we  can use an infinitive after the question words 1  indirect      questions:      Tell    us   what      to   do.

__   who     to   ..., etc   _________________ 111  infinitives       after    question       words: grammar «113     for... to .. .

Robert says he'd be delighted for Emily to come and stay.

I'm eager for the party to be a success.

She's anxious for us to see her work.

adjective + for + object + infinitive future events (e.g. anxious, eager, delighted, willing, reluctant).

express wishes and other personal feelings about the importance or value of The  structure for + object + infinitive can be used after certain adjectives which 3  after    adjectives:       anxious         for    us   to   see    .. .

It's important that the meeting should start on time.

It's important for the meeting to start on time.

generally less formal and often more natural. Compare: as the subject of a clause. It often has the same meaning as a that-clause, but is structures, it is used especially after adjectives, nouns and verbs; it can also act and when we  are giving personal reactions to situations. Like other infinitive frequency, when we  are expressing wishes, suggestions or plans for the future, The  structure is often used when we  are referring to possibility, necessity or 2 use not Anna will be happy for them to help you.
